title: Single Spring-Mass
content: A springmass system exhibits simple harmonic motion (SHM) when damping and external forces are ignored, meaning the only force acting on the mass is a restoring force that pulls it back toward equilibrium. This restoring force follows Hooke’s law, (F = -kx), where (k) is the spring constant and (x) is the displacement from the equilibrium position; the negative sign indicates that the force always acts opposite to the direction of displacement. Applying Newton’s second law leads to the differential equation of motion (m \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = -kx), which can be rewritten as (\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0), the standard form of SHM. The motion is periodic and characterized by an angular frequency (\omega = \sqrt{k/m}), showing that stiffer springs (larger (k)) produce faster oscillations, while heavier masses (larger (m)) result in slower motion. The time period of oscillation is (T = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}), and the frequency is its reciprocal, (f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}). The displacement of the mass as a function of time can be expressed as (x(t) = A\sin(\omega t + \phi)) or equivalently using cosine, where (A) is the amplitude and (\phi) is the phase constant determined by initial conditions. The velocity is obtained by differentiation and varies sinusoidally as well, reaching a maximum value (v_{\max} = \omega A), while the acceleration is proportional to displacement but opposite in direction, given by (a(t) = -\omega^2 x(t)) with maximum magnitude (a_{\max} = \omega^2 A). Energy in an ideal SHM system is conserved and continuously transforms between kinetic and potential forms: at maximum displacement all energy is stored as elastic potential energy, while at the equilibrium position it is entirely kinetic. The total mechanical energy remains constant and is given by (E = \frac{1}{2}kA^2), which depends only on the amplitude and spring constant. An important property of SHM is that the period is independent of amplitude, meaning the oscillation time does not change even if the system is displaced further, as long as the motion remains within the linear regime of the spring. In real systems, damping forces such as friction or air resistance can reduce the amplitude over time, leading to damped harmonic motion, and external driving forces can produce resonance when the driving frequency matches the natural frequency. This model of oscillatory motion is widely applicable, describing systems ranging from mechanical vibrations and vehicle suspensions to molecular and atomic oscillations in physics.

title: Double Spring-Mass
content: In the double spring setup, the block is connected to two springs that both pull toward center. For identical springs, the effective spring constant is k_eff = 2k. The angular frequency becomes omega = sqrt(k_eff/m) = sqrt(2k/m). The period is T = 2 pi sqrt(m/k_eff). Compared with a single spring at the same mass and spring constant value, this setup oscillates faster due to larger effective stiffness. Maximum speed is v_max = omega A and maximum acceleration is a_max = omega^2 A. Energy for the ideal model is E = (1/2) k_eff A^2.

title: Simple Pendulum
content: For small angles, pendulum motion is approximated as simple harmonic. The angular frequency is omega = sqrt(g/L), and the period is T = 2 pi sqrt(L/g). In this approximation, period does not depend on bob mass. The simulation uses a maximum angle control and treats motion close to small-angle behavior. Angular displacement can be modeled as theta(t) = theta0 cos(omega t). Maximum speed near the bottom is approximately v_max = omega L theta0 for small angles in radians. As initial angle grows, exact motion departs from the linear approximation, and period becomes slightly longer than the simple formula.

title: Wave Markers Static Snapshot
content: A static wave snapshot helps identify geometric quantities without time evolution. Amplitude is the maximum displacement from equilibrium line. Wavelength lambda is the distance between two consecutive points in the same phase, such as crest to crest. Crest is the highest point and trough is the lowest point on the waveform. Equilibrium is the undisturbed center line. Frequency and period are linked by f = 1/T. Wave speed in a medium is v = f lambda. This view is useful for recognizing what each symbol means before studying motion over time.

title: Transverse vs Longitudinal
content: Transverse and longitudinal waves differ by direction of particle vibration relative to propagation direction. In a transverse wave, particles oscillate perpendicular to propagation. In a longitudinal wave, particles oscillate parallel to propagation, creating compressions and rarefactions. Both wave types follow the same speed relation v = f lambda in a given medium. Frequency is source-controlled, while wavelength adapts with speed in the medium. Energy is transported even though medium particles mainly oscillate around equilibrium positions. The comparison view is meant to map analogous features between the two representations.

title: Standing Waves on a String
content: Standing waves form from superposition of two waves of equal amplitude and frequency moving in opposite directions. On a string fixed at both ends, displacement must be zero at boundaries, so nodes occur at ends. Antinodes are points of maximum oscillation between nodes. Allowed wavelengths are lambda_n = 2L / n for harmonic number n = 1, 2, 3 and so on. Frequency values are f_n = n v / (2L), where v is wave speed on the string. For a stretched string, wave speed is v = sqrt(T/mu), where T is tension and mu is linear mass density. Increasing tension raises speed and raises harmonic frequencies.

title: Refraction and Snell Law
content: Refraction occurs when light crosses a boundary between media with different refractive indices. Snell law is n1 sin(theta1) = n2 sin(theta2), with angles measured from the normal. Light bends toward the normal when entering a medium with higher refractive index. It bends away from the normal when entering a lower refractive index medium. If n1 > n2 and theta1 exceeds the critical angle, total internal reflection occurs and no refracted ray propagates into medium two. Critical angle obeys sin(theta_c) = n2 / n1 for n1 > n2. Frequency stays constant across boundary while speed and wavelength change.

title: Spherical Mirrors
content: For spherical mirrors in paraxial approximation, mirror formula is 1/f = 1/v + 1/u using a chosen sign convention. Magnification is m = -v/u. A concave mirror can form real and inverted images when object is beyond focal point, and virtual enlarged images when object is between pole and focus. A convex mirror forms virtual, erect, and diminished images for real objects. Principal rays include a ray parallel to principal axis reflecting through focus for concave mirrors, and a ray through focus reflecting parallel to axis. Visual behavior depends strongly on object distance relative to focal length.
